past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 20 years ago, controlled with medication
Chronic constipation, managed conservatively for over 10 years
Remote history of cholecystectomy for gallstones at age 68
family_history: Mother diagnosed with dementia at age 82, died at age 87
Father had ischemic heart disease, died at age 74
One older brother with mild cognitive impairment diagnosed at age 81

social_history.occupation: Retired seamstress, worked in a garment factory for 35 years
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a small apartment since husband passed away 4 years ago; daughter visits twice weekly
social_history.alcohol: Rare, occasional sake with meals, less than once per month
social_history.exercise: Used to attend a local senior walking group until about 8 months ago when she stopped going

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place. Oriented to month and year but states the date is off by five days. MMSE 21/30: lost 3 points on recall (0/3 at 5 minutes), 2 points on orientation (date and day of week), 1 point on serial sevens, 1 point on sentence writing (incomplete thought), and 1 point on figure copy (slight distortion of intersecting pentagons). Clock drawing shows all 12 numbers present but crowded on the right side, with hands placed at an incorrect time (asked for 2:45, drew approximately 3:00). Naming intact for common objects; hesitates on low-frequency items such as 'lapel' and 'buckle'. Repetition intact. Follows simple commands without difficulty but makes an error on a three-step command (omits the last step). Fluency mildly reduced on category testing (animals: 9 in 60 seconds).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Face symmetric. Hearing mildly reduced bilaterally, consistent with age-related presbycusis. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk mildly reduced in bilateral lower extremities consistent with age-related sarcopenia. Tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in upper extremities, 4+/5 in bilateral hip flexors, 5/5 distally in lower extremities. No pronator drift. No tremor or involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch and pinprick in all extremities. Vibration sense mildly diminished at bilateral great toes. Proprioception intact.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 1+ at biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally. 1+ at patellar tendons bilaterally. Achilles reflexes trace bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin mildly unsteady bilaterally but no clear cerebellar pattern. Rapid alternating movements slightly slowed but symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly cautious gait with normal base. Stride length slightly shortened. Tandem gait performed slowly but without significant deviation. Romberg negative. No festination, no freezing.
neurological_exam.additional: No paratonic rigidity. No grasp reflex. No myoclonus. No primitive reflexes elicited. Anosmia not formally tested. Mild kyphotic posture.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive memory loss and difficulty managing daily tasks over the past two years
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Yoshiko Tanaka is a 79-year-old right-handed retired seamstress who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic accompanied by her daughter, Keiko, for evaluation of progressive memory difficulties that have been worsening over the past two years. Her daughter first became concerned approximately two years ago when she noticed her mother was repeating the same questions within a single phone conversation, something that had never happened before. At first, the family attributed this to loneliness following her husband's death four years ago, but the pattern has steadily worsened. Over the past year, her daughter has discovered unpaid utility bills piled in a drawer, and on one occasion the electricity was briefly disconnected before the daughter intervened and began managing her mother's finances. Mrs. Tanaka, who was known among her friends as an exceptionally skilled seamstress capable of complex pattern work, attempted to mend a neighbor's dress six months ago but became confused by the pattern and abandoned the task, something her daughter describes as unprecedented. She has also stopped attending her weekly senior walking group, telling her daughter she cannot remember the meeting time despite having attended the same session for over five years. Her daughter reports that the refrigerator frequently contains expired food, and she has found the same grocery items purchased multiple times on the same day. Three months ago, Mrs. Tanaka called her daughter in distress because she could not remember how to operate the washing machine she has used for fifteen years. She remains able to dress herself, maintain personal hygiene, and prepare simple meals such as rice and miso soup, though her daughter notes the meals have become much less varied than before. Mrs. Tanaka acknowledges that her memory is not as sharp as it used to be and appears embarrassed when her daughter provides examples. She denies episodes of confusion where she did not recognize family members, visual hallucinations, personality changes, or aggressive behavior. She denies headaches, seizures, falls, tremor, or difficulty swallowing. Her sleep pattern has not significantly changed, and her daughter has not witnessed unusual movements or vocalizations during sleep. Her mood has been somewhat low since her husband's passing, but she denies persistent sadness, anhedonia, or suicidal ideation, and she still enjoys watching historical dramas on television.

signal_characteristics.T1: Prominent widening of hippocampal fissures bilaterally with enlargement of temporal horns of lateral ventricles
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No abnormal signal within hippocampal formations

signal_characteristics.Coronal_T1: Widened choroidal fissures and increased perihippocampal CSF spaces bilaterally, right slightly greater than left

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened cortical sulci in temporal and parietal convexities, disproportionate to the patient's age of 79

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Punctate and early confluent periventricular hyperintensities, predominantly caps and pencil-thin lining, consistent with age-appropriate small vessel changes

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy, Scheltens MTA score 3 on the right and 2-3 on the left
volumetrics.ventricular_system: Moderate lateral ventriculomegaly, consistent with ex vacuo dilatation secondary to parenchymal volume loss. Third ventricle mildly prominent.
volumetrics.cortical_thickness: Reduced cortical thickness in bilateral temporal and parietal regions, most pronounced in posterior temporal and inferior parietal lobules

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No evidence of hemorrhage on SWI sequences
Patent major intracranial vessels on MRA without hemodynamically significant stenosis
Post-cholecystectomy changes noted incidentally on lower cuts
mri.impression: Bilateral medial temporal lobe and hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens MTA grade 2-3 left, grade 3 right) with temporoparietal-predominant cortical volume loss disproportionate to the patient's age. Age-appropriate periventricular white matter changes (Fazekas grade 1) without evidence of strategic infarcts or significant vascular burden. No acute intracranial pathology. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall
Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall

output.impression: Abnormal values: MMSE 21/30 points (H), MoCA 16/30 points (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall 3rd percentile percentile (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall 1st percentile percentile (H), Boston Naming Test 20th percentile percentile (H), Category Fluency (Animals) 8th percentile percentile (H), Trail Making Test Part B 15th percentile percentile (H), Geriatric Depression Scale 5/15 points (H).

findings.region: Diffuse cortical uptake, most prominent in precuneus, posterior cingulate, and lateral temporoparietal cortex
findings.signal: Florbetapir_PET: Elevated cortical-to-cerebellar SUVr of 1.48 (positive threshold >1.11); Distribution: Diffuse bilateral cortical amyloid binding with relative sparing of primary sensorimotor and occipital cortices

output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. The elevated SUVr of 1.48 indicates moderate-to-high amyloid burden. In the clinical context of progressive amnestic cognitive decline and supportive structural MRI findings, this result provides strong evidence for a biological diagnosis of Alzheimer disease. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.region: Bilateral temporoparietal cortex and posterior cingulate/precuneus
findings.signal: FDG_PET: Significantly reduced FDG uptake in bilateral temporal and parietal association cortices, posterior cingulate, and precuneus; Pattern: Classic temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism with relative preservation of primary sensorimotor cortex, occipital cortex, basal ganglia, thalamus, and cerebellum

output.impression: Mild prefrontal hypometabolism suggests the disease is transitioning from early to moderate stage. No findings to suggest frontotemporal dementia or Lewy body dementia. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 13 cmH2O. WBC: 2 cells/uL (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 41.2 mg/dL. Glucose: 59.8 mg/dL (ratio 0.64 (serum glucose 94 mg/dL)). Amyloid_beta_42: 348 pg/mL (reference >500 pg/mL) - DECREASED. Amyloid_beta_40: 7850 pg/mL (reference range 5000-12000 pg/mL) - Normal. Amyloid_beta_42_40_ratio: 0.044 (reference >0.065) - DECREASED. Phosphorylated_tau_181: 82 pg/mL (reference <60 pg/mL) - ELEVATED. Total_tau: 520 pg/mL (reference <400 pg/mL) - ELEVATED. 14-3-3_protein: Negative. RT-QuIC: Negative.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-7.5 Hz alpha activity over occipital regions, below the normal range of 8.5-12 Hz

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, did not obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient age and limited cooperation
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photoparoxysmal response. Driving response poorly formed.

output.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG for age. The posterior dominant rhythm is slowed at 7-7.5 Hz with increased diffuse theta activity, most prominent over bilateral temporal regions. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Hyperventilation was not performed. Recording limited to 25-minute awake study.

interactions: Donepezil + Amlodipine: No clinically significant pharmacokinetic interaction
Donepezil + Magnesium oxide: No significant interaction
Donepezil + Calcium carbonate: No significant interaction
warnings: May cause bradycardia; monitor heart rate, especially given concomitant amlodipine (both can lower heart rate)
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, anorexia) are common at initiation and may exacerbate chronic constipation management
Avoid concurrent anticholinergic medications as they may reduce donepezil efficacy
Consider dose escalation to 10 mg after 4-6 weeks if tolerated
alternatives: Rivastigmine transdermal patch - alternative cholinesterase inhibitor, may have fewer GI side effects

output.query: early Alzheimer disease treatment cholinesterase inhibitors 2024

results.title: Cholinesterase inhibitors for Alzheimer's disease: systematic review and meta-analysis
results.summary: Donepezil, rivastigmine, and galantamine produce modest improvements in cognitive function (ADAS-Cog 2-3 point improvement), global impression, and activities of daily living in mild-to-moderate AD. Side effects are predominantly cholinergic (nausea, diarrhea, insomnia). Transdermal rivastigmine may have fewer GI effects.

results.title: Lecanemab in Early Alzheimer's Disease (CLARITY AD)
results.summary: Lecanemab, an anti-amyloid monoclonal antibody, reduced cognitive decline by 27% over 18 months in early AD. Associated with amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA) in 21.3% of patients. Requires biweekly infusions and MRI monitoring. Most benefit seen in APOE e4 non-carriers.

results.title: Nonpharmacological interventions in early-stage Alzheimer's disease: a systematic review
results.summary: Cognitive stimulation therapy, physical exercise programs, and multicomponent interventions show modest benefits for cognition and quality of life in early AD. Caregiver education and support reduce caregiver burden. Structured routines and environmental modifications improve safety.

output.summary: First-line treatment for early AD includes cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, or galantamine), which provide modest cognitive and functional benefits. Anti-amyloid therapies such as lecanemab are emerging but require careful patient selection and monitoring. Nonpharmacological interventions including cognitive stimulation, exercise, caregiver education, and environmental safety modifications are important complementary approaches.

differential.key_distinguishing: MMSE 21/30 is below normal range; functional decline with inability to manage finances, inability to perform previously skilled tasks (sewing), and difficulty with instrumental ADLs confirms dementia beyond normal aging

Gradual insidious onset over 2 years predating any mood symptoms; mood changes are reactive and mild; patient makes effort on cognitive testing; GDS 5/15 borderline and does not account for the severity of cognitive deficits; husband's death was 4 years ago while cognitive decline began 2 years ago

Gradual progressive decline rather than stepwise; only Fazekas grade 1 white matter changes on MRI without strategic infarcts; memory-predominant pattern rather than executive-predominant; no history of stroke or TIA
No visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism on examination (no rigidity, no resting tremor), no REM sleep behavior disorder, no fluctuating cognition
No behavioral or personality changes; no disinhibition; memory impairment is the predominant symptom; preserved social comportment; age of onset typical for AD rather than FTD

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and informant, including timeline of cognitive decline, nature of complaints, functional status, and behavioral symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive amnestic cognitive decline over 2 years with functional impairment confirmed by daughter, including loss of ability to manage finances, inability to perform previously skilled tasks, and difficulty with instrumental ADLs

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with detailed mental status assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: MMSE 21/30 with prominent recall deficit (0/3 at 5 minutes), impaired clock drawing, reduced category fluency; neurological exam otherwise unremarkable for focal deficits

optimal_actions.action: Order dementia screening laboratory studies: CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate, RPR, HIV
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All values within normal limits, ruling out reversible causes of cognitive decline

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2-3 to 3) with temporoparietal-predominant cortical volume loss, minimal vascular burden
Amnestic profile with severely impaired delayed recall, reduced semantic fluency and confrontation naming, consistent with AD

optimal_actions.action: Consider CSF biomarkers or amyloid PET for pathological confirmation of Alzheimer disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Decreased Abeta42, elevated phospho-tau and total tau, confirming AD pathology (A+T+N+)

optimal_actions.action: Initiate cholinesterase inhibitor therapy (donepezil 5 mg daily) after checking drug interactions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions with current medications

optimal_actions.action: Provide diagnosis disclosure and counseling to patient and family, including discussion of prognosis, safety planning, advance directives, and community resources
Assess safety: evaluate living alone status, kitchen safety, medication management, and whether increased supervision is needed
Schedule follow-up in 3-6 months to monitor treatment response and disease progression

critical_actions: Exclude reversible causes of dementia with laboratory studies (TSH, B12, RPR, HIV)
Perform standardized cognitive assessment (MMSE, MoCA, or neuropsychological battery)
Obtain collateral history from family member or informant
Assess functional status and safety, especially given patient lives alone
Discuss diagnosis, prognosis, and safety planning with patient and family
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, tricyclic antidepressants) which worsen cognitive function in dementia
Prescribing benzodiazepines for anxiety without careful consideration of fall risk and cognitive side effects in elderly dementia patients
Attributing cognitive symptoms to grief or normal aging without formal evaluation
Starting memantine monotherapy in mild disease (not indicated until moderate-to-severe stage)
Ordering brain biopsy (not indicated with this clinical and biomarker profile)
Prescribing antipsychotics without clear behavioral indication (risk of increased mortality in dementia patients)
key_reasoning_points: Progressive episodic memory loss over 2 years with insidious onset is the hallmark presentation of early Alzheimer disease

Functional decline including inability to manage finances, abandoning a previously mastered skilled task (sewing), and difficulty with instrumental ADLs distinguishes dementia from mild cognitive impairment
MMSE 21/30 with disproportionate recall deficit (0/3 at 5 minutes) and impaired clock drawing is characteristic of hippocampal-type amnesia seen in AD
Normal neurological examination aside from cognitive deficits argues against vascular dementia, Lewy body dementia, and frontotemporal dementia
Family history of dementia in mother and cognitive impairment in brother are risk factors supporting the diagnosis

MRI showing bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2-3 to 3) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss and only Fazekas grade 1 white matter changes supports a primary neurodegenerative rather than vascular etiology
The patient lives alone, which is a critical safety consideration requiring specific counseling about supervision, home modifications, and future care planning

The source case described an elderly woman with established Alzheimer's dementia in a nursing home, and this case captures the earlier stage of the disease trajectory with a classic amnestic presentation in an outpatient setting

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic progressive amnestic presentation over 2 years, prominent functional decline, supportive family history, consistent MRI findings showing bilateral hippocampal atrophy, and absence of features suggesting alternative diagnoses
